perturbation.original_text: All such taxes or duties shall be for the account of Distributor and any such taxes or duties required to be paid or collected by NETGEAR shall be paid by Distributor to NETGEAR unless Distributor provides NETGEAR with a valid certificate of exemption acceptable to the appropriate taxing or governmental authority.
perturbation.changed_text: All such taxes or duties shall be generally for the account of Distributor, and taxes or duties NETGEAR attempts to collect shall be paid by Distributor to NETGEAR unless Distributor provides what it believes to be a certificate of exemption.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the distributor is responsible for all taxes and duties unless a valid exemption certificate is provided and accepted by the relevant authority. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'generally' and 'what it believes to be,' suggesting the distributor's responsibility is not absolute and that their own assessment of the certificate's validity is sufficient. This creates uncertainty and potential disputes over tax obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6091
perturbation.law_explanation: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6091 states that all gross receipts are presumed to be subject to tax until the contrary is established. It also places the burden of proving otherwise on the seller unless the seller takes a certificate in good faith from the purchaser that the property is purchased for resale. By allowing the distributor to provide 'what it believes to be' a certificate of exemption, the modified contract language contradicts the requirement for a 'valid certificate' as determined by the appropriate taxing authority. In essence, the law requires the seller to have a certificate that meets legal standards to avoid liability, while the modified contract allows the distributor's subjective belief to suffice, potentially shifting tax responsibilities incorrectly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, RTC 6091 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Revenue and Taxation Code - RTC DIVISION 2. OTHER TAXES [6001 - 61050] ( Heading of Division 2 amended by Stats. 1968, Ch. 279. ) PART 1. SALES AND USE TAXES [6001 - 7176] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 36. ) CHAPTER 2. The Sales Tax [6051 - 6172] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 36. ) ARTICLE 3. Presumptions and Resale Certificates [6091 - 6095] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 36. ) 6091. For the purpose of the proper administration of this part and to prevent evasion of the sales tax it shall be presumed that all gross receipts are subject to the tax until the contrary is established. The burden of proving that a sale of tangible personal property is not a sale at retail is upon the person who makes the sale unless he takes from the purchaser a certificate to the effect that the property is purchased for resale. (Added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 36.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6091. The original text requires a 'valid certificate of exemption acceptable to the appropriate taxing or governmental authority'. The changed text allows the distributor to provide 'what it believes to be a certificate of exemption', which weakens the requirement for a valid certificate determined by the taxing authority. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, RTC 6091, it is presumed that all gross receipts are subject to tax until proven otherwise, and the burden of proof lies on the seller unless they obtain a certificate from the purchaser for resale. The modified contract language, by allowing a subjective belief about the certificate's validity, undermines the legal standard for exemption, as the certificate's validity is determined by the taxing authority, not the distributor's belief.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to maximize sales of NETGEAR Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor agrees to attempt to use commercially reasonable efforts to increase sales of NETGEAR Products.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the distributor to use commercially reasonable efforts to maximize sales. This is a standard business obligation. The modified text weakens this obligation by including the word 'attempt,' making the obligation to maximize sales less definitive and more discretionary. The distributor is only obligated to attempt to use commercially reasonable efforts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including unfair or fraudulent business acts or practices. While a simple contract breach is not automatically an unfair business practice, systematically failing to use reasonable efforts to maximize sales (as now ambiguously permitted by the added 'attempt') when contractually obligated, with the intent to harm NETGEAR's market share or benefit a competing product, could be construed as an unfair business practice. The modification weakens the obligation to a point where fulfilling it becomes optional, potentially creating a scenario of unfair competition if the distributor intentionally sabotages sales, violating the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maximize sales by adding the word "attempt". While California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including unfair or fraudulent business acts or practices, the addition of "attempt" introduces ambiguity, making the obligation less definitive and more discretionary. The scraped law snippet 1 defines unfair competition as "any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice". By weakening the obligation to maximize sales, the distributor could potentially engage in actions that, while not explicitly a breach, could be viewed as an unfair business practice if the distributor intentionally sabotages sales.

perturbation.original_text: NETGEAR and Distributor agree to comply with the provisions of all applicable federal, state, county and local laws, ordinances, regulations and codes, domestic and foreign.
perturbation.changed_text: NETGEAR and Distributor generally agree to comply with what they understand to be the provisions of applicable laws, ordinances, regulations and codes, domestic and foreign.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear and unambiguous legal obligation to comply with all applicable laws. The modified text adds ambiguity by using 'generally agree' and 'what they understand to be,' making compliance subjective and discretionary. Each party is now only obligated to comply with laws to the extent they understand them, creating a loophole for non-compliance based on a claimed lack of understanding or a disagreement about the interpretation of the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 1904.5(a) requires employers to record work-related injuries and illnesses. By adding 'what they understand to be', the modified contract language allows either party to claim ignorance of OSHA regulations, potentially leading to underreporting of workplace incidents. This ambiguity contradicts the strict reporting requirements mandated by OSHA and undermines the goal of workplace safety by allowing for subjective interpretation of compliance, making it unenforceable and putting employee safety at risk. The objective standard of the law is replaced with a subjective one.
perturbation.location: 18. C. NETGEAR and Distributor agree to comply with the provisions of all applicable federal, state, county and local laws, ordinances, regulations and codes, domestic and foreign.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1904.5 - Determination of work-relatedness. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1904.5 - Determination of work-relatedness. Part Number: 1904 Part Number Title: Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses Subpart: 1904 Subpart C Subpart Title: Recordkeeping Forms and Recording Criteria Standard Number: 1904.5 Title: Determination of work-relatedness. GPO Source: e-CFR 1904.5(a) Basic requirement. You must consider an injury or illness to be work-related if an event or exposure in the work environment either caused or contributed to the resulting condition or significantly aggravated a pre-existing injury or illness. Work-relatedness is presumed for injuries and illnesses resulting from events or exposures occurring in the work environment, unless an exception in §1904.5(b)(2) specifically applies 1904.5(b) Implementation. 1904.5(b)(1) What is the "work environment"? OSHA defines the work environment as "the establishment and other locations where one or more employees are working or are present as a condition of their employment. The work environment includes not only physical locations, but also the equipment or materials used by the employee during the course of his or her work." 1904.5(b)(2) Are there situations where an injury or illness occurs in the work environment and is not considered work-related? Yes, an injury or illness occurring in the work environment that falls under one of the following exceptions is not work-related, and therefore is not recordable. 1904.5(b)(2) You are not required to record injuries and illnesses if . . . (i) At the time of the injury or illness, the employee was present in the work environment as a member of the general public rather than as an employee. (ii) The injury or illness involves signs or symptoms that surface at work but result solely from a non-work-related event or exposure that occurs outside the work environment. (iii) The injury or illness results
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the original obligation to comply with all applicable laws. By using 'generally agree' and 'what they understand to be,' the contract allows for subjective interpretation and potential non-compliance. The Law Explanation argues this undermines workplace safety by potentially leading to underreporting. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 1904.5(a), establishes a 'basic requirement' to consider an injury or illness work-related if an event or exposure in the work environment either caused or contributed to the resulting condition. The change in the contract allows parties to claim a lack of understanding of OSHA regulations, which would directly contradict the strict reporting requirements of 1904.5(a).
